speaker: interviewer
text: How much time did you spend in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 To 11 weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about your experience there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz? You saw the-- every day, day and night, the chimney was smoking from fire and things. Everything went there-- people. When they burned the people, they gassed the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see--

speaker: interviewee
text: You feel the smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any selections when you were there? Did they have selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was selections for me, too. I was there in a selection, too. Yeah, when I came to Auschwitz, they put me on-- they called it a place not to work. I don't know. It was for the Red Cross something. I have no idea. The thing is that they put me in a room, in a barrack. It was just sick people. And I was over there, maybe, two weeks. A guy came to me, he knew me-- he was before over there, he was longer in Auschwitz. He told me, Pincus, when this cut, you have to go out. Sooner is better. Once in a while they come in the night. They take out all the clean up, all the people, they put in the crematorium. So I try-- I was lucky. I went out, and I went   down to second block. And they took me in.

words.text: It was for the Red Cross

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a couple days, and this has happened, they took out all the people from over there. And later, when I was over there, in Auschwitz they make selections every couple days. They didn't took out too many people, but-- [NON-ENGLISH]. So I was the lucky one. I was able to go out in Auschwitz to Dachau-Kaufering. When did you leave Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think was November, '44. November or December, something. Just-- but before they know the Russian had come in the north.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Kaufering. This is apart from Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of a camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work, always they found work for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, well, not every day-- was building something. It was all kind of work, they got. They built something. They took us-- even you work so much, over there, you couldn't stay in the camp, just to go out. Once, I think, they liquidate one camp, they took us to take out all the potatoes in the garden there. And over there, all food to be, now, a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there something that happened to you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what happened like-- I was in-- well, I worked in the place. We worked someplace, we worked away. I don't know. I was accidentally something. So we couldn't find back to our group. So we saw it was very bad. We was worried. If they catch you, they say that you runned away. So I saw an SS man from far away. So I run to him. He shouldn't think I run away. And I told him the story, what's happened to us. So he said, no. Come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with him-- I am with a friend, too, was there. And we was there. They know-- they just saw we want to run away. That's allthat. And I told him. Didn't help. So they took an SS man, and he should take me back to work. And I know if the SS man comes with me, and with my friend, and they say they runned away-- like they said over there-- they can hang me for this. So all the way we walked, maybe a half hour. He was an older-- he was not one of the young guys. He was, maybe, in the 40s, late 40s. And I told him, you have children, too, maybe. And I didn't mean to run away. My life is in your hands. And it depends what you say. So the whole way, I talk. He didn't answer me a word. He didn't say he could help me or not. Later when we come in the place, he pushed me back to the work, and he went away. So I was lucky. How long did you stay in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, bad conditions, really bad conditions. It's unbelievable to tell people, what was in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep in a barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in a bed. I mean, but they make this, you know, from wood. No straw, even-- just hard on the wood. Well, the thing is this, who was young could survive. The most thing was good, even winter, you have to go in and wash yourself-- keep clean, how far you can. You couldn't keep clean, but this helps. Well, I met over there in this camp, too, a younger guy from my city. And I didn't recognize him. He recognized me. So he helped me. He came back with barrack. He tried to give me better work. So he helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any violence when you were in this camp? Did you see any punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the SS? Always. Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you stand in the appell, the morning before to go to work. If you couldn't stay straight, something, they hit you with a-- right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any executions, any hangings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I saw execution, hanging, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be the reason that someone would have been executed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was before-- this was in Sosnowiec, I saw. People, everything was on carts, like food, bread, material. One guy stole some bread tickets, a coupon, a ticket for bread. And they caught him. They hanged him for this. Middle of the street, people have to go and see it. Also, wasn't in Kaufering, in the Dachau lager. A guy in-- Russian guy-- ranned away. They catched him, they killed him. They brought him and show us. He lay on the floor. It was hard to run away. And if you could run away, it was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, you thinking-- if you in a camp like this-- you think just day by day, to survive. And I also think, if I survive, I don't know. Maybe I didn't want to be any more, the Jewish people. I want to go tell, I'm not Jewish. That's what I was thinking. I saw what they done to the Jewish people. Later, I changed my mind. If you got free, you saw more Jews. You started talking, so you changed your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau? Almost a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, when the American assault come, and they know they come. And they took out all the people in the camp. And we walked. I walked with them for two days. And they told us they might kill us. So after two days, we walked-- the airplanes started to come, the American airplanes. And so I see is a chance to run away. And I was in the woods, by the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: So me and my friend, we prepared before, right away. We should, if we had an occasion, we run away. So we both run away in the woods. We was four days, in the woods. We took a little food, start, beginning. It was too late. I mean, the time went, four days, and we was hungry. We have no choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, in this camp, I didn't got a uniform with stripes. We took regular jackets. And the back, there was make a window, a little window. They put in white, in case you ran away. They should recognize you. But before I went, I prepared me a needle and thread. I took from a tailor, and I told him-- I didn't tell him what I want to do. So when we was in the woods, sleeping in the night, the four days, I cut off this. And I sew this back on, and for the cutout from the side a piece of material. And I put back in, so nobody could recognize me if I go out. So after four days, we went out. And it was a farmers, so nobody knows. I'm Jewish, I'm Catholic, am I Polish. We just asked for food. They give us food. So this way. After a couple days-- two, three days-- we got freed by the Americans. Was 20, 30 miles before Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about the day that you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was happy. It was maybe-- I was-- so many people, just Jewish would run away, too, other places. So it was unbelievable. Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that you were free, what was the first thing that you wanted to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I eat enough. We care for food. Later, we want to find out-- we wants find somebody, maybe from the family. I always wish I don't want to be in camp with my brother, somebody. See how they're hungry, you know? Later, you could go back. Was not easy, from the beginning, was transportation was very hard. You could go back to Poland, find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to myself, I never go back to Poland. What would be-- my foot will never go to Poland. So I never went to Poland. In the place that I was over there, we went to Munich. Was not far, maybe 30, 40 miles to Munich. I don't know. I went-- we worked. I don't know about now. So I met a guy from my city. And he told me, Pincus, you know you got three brothers up there. They're living. And he [INAUDIBLE], because he told me they are now. And so I go back to where I live and I packed everything, and I went. And it took me a couple day. It was not easy. It was not easy, transportation. So I met three brothers and a sister-in-law. Did they know that you had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody knows from each other. One brother what lives in Israel, he saw on the list. And he came, like I came, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, not in one place. I was in Germany from '45 to '51-- end of '51-- six years. And Germany helped me-- I make chocolate. I got a chocolate factory in Germany. So I told there-- I got people by the work bus. I saw this man. I told him-- his name was Miller-- he told me, Pincus, why you going to Germany? To America, you will work hard, over there. Here, you've got it good. I told him, this is not my place, to stay here. I don't want my children should grow up in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I try-- it was not easy to go away. I went, too, in Germany, plenty with my wife. She was [INAUDIBLE]. So I went to-- it didn't easy to go to America. I bought, already, a Frigidaire-- never think that-- and I sent it already to Israel. I really want to go to Israel. He was thinking Israel. So my youngest brother, he went first again. So we went in the camp, he went to Israel, the first. He wasn't [NON-ENGLISH] all right. They took him. And later, he came to Israel and he sent me a letter-- I know Bluma is sick, my wife is sick. He told me, before you come to Israel, stop in America. See? So I went to America. And this way was not easy with my wife. You were married in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started a family in Germany? You started your family in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some of them, yeah. My father, he would want I should marry in Poland, with my wife. But I didn't want, this time. I got another girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father knew your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, yeah. And my mother, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you think your parents would feel, if they knew that you married her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, very happy, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had two daughters in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two daughters in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther and Gitta. Gitta isn't my name. My mother's name, Gitta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America, yeah, 1957. We got there, Rosie was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do here? How did you earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: From here, from the beginning, soon as I can I went right away, to work. I worked very hard. I worked very hard all the time, until I retired. I was special over there. I was sweeping floors in a factory. And I was happy. I didn't want to be in Germany-- better this way. So I make maybe $30 a week, this time was everything cheap-- not as high prices. So we could save up a couple dollars from this. I even sent packages to Israel, to my brother. And I started to work hard. My wife wants to take a job. I said, by me, you will not work. You're sitting home. She even-- the people here, in America, they-- from the beginning-- they took in boarders. You know, the boarders-- I told my wife, in my house, a boarder don't come in, in my house-- not a woman, not a man. So I work hard. I work nine months, very hard. Later, I started the business-- a dry cleaning business. It was very hard. I didn't make much. I might make more by work. This time, when I quit the job, I make over $100 a week, already. So I started my business work, I make $45, $30. Later it worked out. But in the beginning is hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your life since you came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this country, I was very happy, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you happy now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very happy, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have three daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Cannot be better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren, 10 grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were raising your children, did you tell them about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not in the beginning. My wife start talk to them. They sometimes didn't want to listen. They was young. But later, they was involved. They know, already, about things. And I told them about things. My wife tried to brainwash them to go to the university, try to find Jewish boys-- not other people. They was brainwashed. I mean, really. And they got nice-- I got nice son-in-laws. Cannot be better, even-- like my wife said, if she would hold them as babies at home, she wouldn't be better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still think about the war today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day. I don't forget what I went through. I cannot forget this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have any dreams or nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty dreams, sometimes I get up sick. Sometimes I get up in the middle of the night, so sick I have to read a paper, sit down for a half hour. If you fall, right away, sleep farther, it comes back the same thing as when you think. I rest a half hour, they're different. Sometimes I take a tablet for sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel you're able to enjoy everything life has to offer, today? Are you able to enjoy yourself? If you go to a wedding, do you feel happy or do you feel sad?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, if I go to any wedding, or any place, I always remember with my parents, and the Jewish people, what's happened them. I never forget this. I have to take it in, like I tell you-- same thing. When is a wedding, people breaking a glass. You got to remember this. It's hard to forget. People where they was together, and everything. Not just my parents, my brothers, or other people-- how hard they worked in Poland. Not easy-- it was a very hard life. Here is difference. Here, you work hard, you get something. You got something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think it's important that you made this tape today? What made you decide to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: The future, the children, the grandchildren, should remember what was happened to us. I think this will be a good thing, a beautiful thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been 50 years now, since your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over 50 years. In May it was 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any last message that you would like to give to your children who will be seeing this tape, or your grandchildren, anything that you'd like to say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: About this tape, I didn't think. I teach my children, if you have-- you can help people. The nation give-- and they do this. You see, I show them I'm going to work, even when I'm tired. And I'm so happy they call me. But they never gave me a raise. I've worked over there, so happy. They honor me a couple of times, already, for this. I even pick up people to work, when I go the same way, on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you feel it's important to give something back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the number that I got in Auschwitz-- B5566. This is two of my brothers-- one this side, and one on this side. I'm in the middle. The older brother is Leo, and the other brother is Mendel, the other brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schwandorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, excuse me. Can I say--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my wife in Schwandorf after we were married. In Schwandorf, me and my wife, with my daughter Esther. Me and my wife in Munich, in 1950. This picture is made, 1960-- me and my three daughters, Esther, Gitta, and Rosie. This is [INAUDIBLE] we donated-- me and my friends-- we bought, we sent to Israel. So we was in Israel, and we make the picture-- me, and my wife, and our cousin. This is a picture. They honor me with-- I was a president [INAUDIBLE]. So there, Mr. [INAUDIBLE] took it over. This was in Jewish center, they make the-- they honor me. I went -- they invited me, with my children, and my wife, and the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was that dinner. It was a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a dinner, yeah. This is a picture what I wanted all the grandchildren should be together. The name from the grandchildren is Lauren, Devorah, Sari, [INAUDIBLE], Tamar, Aaron, Alex, Elana, Tamar, Robbie. This is my beautiful family-- my wife, Bluma; my daughter, Esther; my daughter, Gitta; and Rosie. The grandchildren from Esther is Sari. The grandchildren from Gitta is Tamar, Aaron, Alex. And the grandchildren from Rosie is Elana, Mara, Robbie.

speaker: interviewee
text: On behalf of my sister Gitta and Rosie, I'd like to say a few words. The three of us have had a very intimate relationship with our grandparents, who are eyewitnesses to the Holocaust. And we can attest to their struggle after the war. They worked very hard, and didn't want to be a burden to society or to their families. They achieved success because they had a lot of emotional strength. They're not bitter. They didn't pass that on to us. They didn't burden us with their sufferings. And they brought us up with a lot of optimism. And we are forever grateful that we have had such wonderful parents and grandparents. My two older daughters, Lauren and Devorah, can't be here, so I wanted to read just a couple things that they have written. This is part of an essay that Lauren wrote, recently. 'The mathematics that inspired my global humanitarian concerns are found, not printed on the pages of a textbook, but rather in the numbers branded on the forearms of my grandmother and grandfather. They have told me about Auschwitz and Dachau, about the nightmares that plague them 50 years later, about the irrational deaths of a doting father, a heroic big brother, and an innocent baby sister. They have elicited my deepest sorrow and most hardened anger. And they have instilled a determination in me to avoid the irresponsible ignorance and treacherous apathy that allowed their inhumane suffering.' And from Devorah, 'I admire my grandparents for all the warmth and love they offer to me and the entire family, in spite of all the suffering they lived through when they were my age. They inspired me to work hard and appreciate what the world has to offer. I love them very much.' MICHAEL (SON-IN-LAW): I'm the son-in-law, Michael. And all I can say is that they have given us a perspective to appreciate life, and appreciate what's really important. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're all very proud of our grandparents, and I love them very much. Well, I think it's a miracle that they survived the Holocaust. And if they didn't, we wouldn't be here talking today. And I love them very much, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my grandparents are very brave for what they've been through with the Holocaust, and I love them very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my grandparents very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love our grandma and grandpa very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're very grateful and very lucky that we survived, that we can celebrate our 50 anniversary. Some couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Robbie's here. Shabbat shalom. Say Shabbat shalom. Loud. Say--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you say, hello Papa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say Bluma. Papa, Pinky.
